briefings.headline: Former nun details 12 years inside a Carmelite monastery in new memoir
briefings.body: Catherine Coldstream entered a Carmelite monastery at age 24 and spent 12 years living a cloistered life of prayer and obedience before leaving. She recounts her experience in her memoir, Cloistered: My Years as a Nun. Her account offers a rare firsthand perspective on life inside a contemplative religious community and the personal journey that led her to eventually walk away.

sources.title: A former nun explains why she ran away from her 'Cloistered' life

briefings.why_it_matters: First-person accounts of cloistered religious life are rarely made public, and Coldstream's memoir could offer readers an unusually candid window into an institution that affects the lives of thousands of people who enter or consider entering monastic communities.

briefings.headline: West Virginia's paid relocation program is turning temporary remote workers into permanent residents
briefings.body: West Virginia's Ascend program, launched during COVID, offered cash incentives to attract remote workers to the state. The initiative is one of dozens of similar relocation programs across the country aimed at providing economic boosts to regions struggling with population and workforce decline. Many participants who originally relocated temporarily have chosen to stay, suggesting these incentive-based programs could be an effective tool for reversing population loss.

sources.title: West Virginia paid digital nomads to move there during COVID. Many are staying

briefings.why_it_matters: For remote workers weighing where to live, paid relocation programs could offer financial support while the communities offering them could see lasting economic and demographic benefits.

briefings.headline: Justice Department drops New York Times subpoenas after judicial pushback
briefings.body: The Justice Department has withdrawn subpoenas seeking grand jury testimony and phone records from New York Times journalists, retreating after a federal judge sharply questioned the government's position during a Thursday hearing. The move ends an immediate legal threat to the reporters' sources and communications.

sources.title: Justice Dept. drops 'New York Times' subpoenas under pressure from judge

briefings.why_it_matters: When the government backs down from compelling journalists to testify or hand over records, it could help protect the confidential sources that reporters rely on to bring sensitive information to the public.

briefings.headline: Best friends discover they are biological siblings after years of close friendship
briefings.body: Andrea Urmston had long known she had a brother placed with another family through adoption, but had no idea he was already her closest friend. The two had formed a tight bond before uncovering their biological connection. Their story highlights how adoption records and limited family information can leave siblings unaware of each other's existence, sometimes for decades, even when living in close proximity.

sources.title: They became best friends - then discovered they were brother and sister

briefings.why_it_matters: For the estimated millions of people separated from siblings through adoption, this case underscores how accessible DNA testing and open adoption records could help families identify connections hiding in plain sight.

briefings.headline: Schools Face a Growing Crisis in Teaching Students to Write
briefings.body: As schools have made progress on reading instruction, writing has emerged as the next major literacy challenge. At Sand Creek Elementary in Highlands Ranch, Colorado, teachers are working to explicitly teach students how to compose written work, with some reporting stronger student growth than in previous years. Research suggests that writing instruction has long been inconsistent or undertaught in American classrooms, leaving many students underprepared for academic and professional demands.

sources.title: The next literacy challenge: Teaching students how to write

briefings.why_it_matters: Weak writing skills could limit students' academic achievement and career prospects, making the quality of writing instruction in local schools a concern for parents and communities alike.

briefings.headline: Rayner Rules Out Rent Controls in England
briefings.body: Housing Secretary Angela Rayner has told the BBC that rent controls will not be introduced in England, arguing that the Renters Rights Act is already having a significant impact on the housing market. Rayner indicated the government is relying on existing legislation rather than price caps to address affordability concerns for tenants across England.

sources.title: Angela Rayner rules out rent controls in England

briefings.why_it_matters: Renters hoping for government-imposed limits on what landlords can charge will need to look to other measures for relief, as rent levels in England could continue to be set by market forces.

quick_hits.text: Oil hit $100 per barrel after Houthi rebels attacked two Saudi tankers, while Trump simultaneously rolled out a new round of tariffs.

source.title: Oil surges to $100 per barrel. And, Trump imposes a new round of tariffs

source.title: Google hit with $1 billion in fines as EU braces for Trump battle

source.title: New York Times lawyer talks about DOJ withdrawing subpoenas sent to Times' reporters

source.title: Using a bathroom 20 times a day - life in the middle of US cyclospora outbreak

voice_blocks.title: This Day
voice_blocks.text: 2025: Angara Airlines Flight 2311 crashes on approach to Tynda Airport, killing all 48 people on board.

closer.text: "Let the people know the facts, and the country will be safe."
